item.title: Germany’s Role in a Post-American Europe
item.description: America’s rejection of transatlanticism will dramatically change Europe internally. Whether the continent will persist as an autonomous political entity now depends on Germany's ability to preserve the spirit of cooperation that animated the European project when the United States was still on board.

item.title: How Bombing Iran May Blow Back on America
item.description: The fact that Donald Trump could unleash the immense power of the US military against Iran with brazen disregard for facts and law will only embolden him to use such powers again. Trailing badly in opinion polls, will he use the backdrop of violence in Iran to justify domestic repression in the run-up to the mid-term elections?

item.title: The War on Iran and the War on Anthropic
item.description: US President Donald Trump, who came to power promising no new foreign military entanglements, especially in the Middle East, has now launched a potentially riskier one than the Iraq War a generation ago, and with even flimsier justification. But his use of state power to crush a private company may have even more far-reaching effects.

item.title: Ayatollah Khamenei’s Deadly Legacy
item.description: Ayatollah Ali Khamenei’s authority rested on a mix of religious legitimacy, military loyalty, and economic control, all anchored within constitutional mechanisms that concentrate power while preserving the outward appearance of legality. This authoritarian system will most likely prevent genuine, lasting change in Iran.

item.title: The Perils of a Power Vacuum in Iran
item.description: The US sees regimes it can strike and concludes that striking them resolves the dangers they pose. But eliminating a visible adversary does not neutralize the underlying threat; it merely transforms that threat into something elusive, decentralized, unaccountable, and impossible to negotiate with or monitor.

item.title: Trump’s Risky War of Choice in Iran
item.description: Once again, the US has opted to make a massive strategic commitment in the Middle East. But while it takes only one side to begin a war, it takes two to end it – and Iran now has a vote in how big this conflict becomes and how long it continues.

item.title: British Lessons About American Decline
item.description: Rather than indulging in anti-immigration culture wars and tarring Britain as a case study in "civilizational erasure," Donald Trump and his followers’ would do well to examine how Britain struggled with the loss of its own global preeminence. No other country’s recent past better represents America’s possible future.

item.title: Trump’s Shaky Bargains
item.description: Positional bargaining might work for real-estate transactions, but it is woefully ill-suited to complex diplomatic challenges. Negotiations like those over the Ukraine war and Iran’s nuclear ambitions should aim not to settle the terms of a one-off exchange, but to build a framework that can shape and manage future developments.

item.title: Global Health Financing Needs a Reset
item.description: With major donor countries slashing their foreign-aid budgets, the financial foundations of global health have come under strain, revealing structural weaknesses long masked by steady public funding. The solution lies in building a more resilient system that combines grants, concessional loans, and private capital.

item.title: The 6G Race Will Define the Next Digital Order
item.description: The next generation of mobile technology will determine who controls the critical infrastructure on which modern economies, security systems, and democratic governance depend. The stakes in the race to shape the sector's technical standards could not be higher, for developed and developing countries alike.

item.title: AI and the End of Time
item.description: For more than two centuries, time has been capitalism’s organizing principle, structuring wages, contracts, and collective action. But AI has made human time functionally abundant, undermining that foundation and fracturing labor into three incompatible regimes no single legal system can reconcile.

item.title: The New Age of Resource Competition Needs Transparency
item.description: As countries scramble to secure the minerals needed for clean energy, digital technologies, AI, and defense industries, the new era of resource competition implies both great promise and grave peril. Transparency is essential to ensure that producer countries are not left poorer, more divided, and more indebted than before.

item.title: Christians vs. America’s Taliban
item.description: With both the Holocaust denier Nick Fuentes and more genteel post-liberal commentators calling for their own religious views to be imposed on others, traditional conservatives and Christians must speak up. The state has no business using coercion in matters of conscience, as the Catholic Church itself has long made clear.

item.title: What Trump Gets Wrong About Corruption and Business
item.description: As US President Donald Trump’s pay-to-play approach to politics blurs the line between legal and illegal, businesses may feel pressured to go along. But decades of evidence show that corruption raises capital costs, depresses growth, distorts incentives, and ultimately hardens into strategic decay.

item.title: Jeffrey Epstein and the Perils of Anti-Elitism
item.description: The files of the convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein have shone a light on the potential misconduct of powerful elites, which should be investigated. But the daily dose of allegations, accusations, and scurrilous gossip risks triggering a moral panic that could intensify calls for strongman rule.

item.title: What Will the Fed’s “Warsh Era” Bring?
item.description: The next Federal Reserve chair will inherit an institution that, by any historical measure, is deeply fractured internally and lacks credibility externally. By strengthening the Fed’s analytical framework and establishing a "balance-sheet theory," Kevin Warsh can oversee the institution's return to excellence.

item.title: Should Central Banks Hold Bitcoin Reserves?
item.description: As geopolitical tensions rise, central banks are intensifying efforts to reduce dependence on the US dollar. While Bitcoin’s appeal as a politically neutral reserve asset is growing, it remains highly volatile and lacks the long track record that underpins gold’s status as the ultimate safe haven.

item.title: The State of Trump
item.description: US President Donald Trump’s State of the Union address showed a man who is stuck, exhausted, and out of options. To move beyond the current system of competitive authoritarianism to full-blown fascism, he must give the country a war that it does not want, and then win – but that is out of his reach.

item.title: The Supreme Court’s Tariff Ruling Changes Little
item.description: The US Supreme Court’s ruling on Donald Trump’s tariffs may modify the legal facade of US trade policy, as well as produce a brief surge of imports into the United States. But, ultimately, it is unlikely to reduce the overall height or significantly alter the country composition of US trade barriers.

item.title: Stablecoins Are Raising New Risks for Emerging Markets
item.description: Stablecoins are rapidly evolving from payment instruments into full-fledged financial infrastructure in Africa, Asia, and Latin America, introducing risks that the US-centric debate has largely overlooked. As parallel payment systems grow beyond the scope of existing banking rules, policymakers must expand the regulatory perimeter.
